data.title: SoundHound AI: Moats Are Resonating, But The Premium Is Deafening (Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

This would mark the third time I’m covering SoundHound AI, Inc. (SOUN), one of those companies trying to make talking to machines feel natural, like talking to a person instead of pressing buttons or yelling at an electronic-sounding voice assistant. Basically, they build software that lets businesses add really smart voice assistants into their products like in drive-throughts, cars, and customer service lines.

Last year when I covered the stock, in terms of market sentiment, we had a lot of animal spirits and FOMO, which tends to be a red flag for me for investments like this. Plus, to be honest, while the tech itself seemed impressive, it reminded me of a product like GoPro, Inc. (GPRO), which came out of the gates super hot a little more than a decade ago, but when you strip away the curtain, it was just a convenient camera wrapped in a protective casing. In other words, it was a product that you could easily clone or replicate in various forms. And look at GPRO today, it’s a penny stock, and that was really my thought process with SOUN, which, by the looks of it, has been graced by Seeking Alpha’s red label “high risk” warning.

In hindsight, I gave the momentum crowd too much credit; after my last Hold call, the stock had a brief pop, then settled into a steady grind down. Today, after news of the CFO’s departure, I reviewed the latest numbers and moved from neutral to bearish, as the trade now is mostly risk, with the margin for error wiped out.

SoundHound AI, Inc. Growth Drivers and Financial Position

  • FY 2025 Revenue: $169M (+99% YoY)
  • Q4 2025 Revenue: $55.1M (+59% YoY).

Over the entire year, SoundHound’s total revenue nearly doubled compared to the previous year. In the fourth quarter alone, revenue increased by 59%. And the gains didn’t come from a single hot streak but from new business landing in multiple places at once. The company says enterprise AI is where things are clicking most, particularly in healthcare and finance. In these industries, businesses are dealing with higher labor costs and more complicated customer service needs.

  • Automotive MAU Growth: 50%+ YoY
  • Automotive Query Growth: ~75% YoY.

The automotive part also continued to grow, especially in faster-growing Asian markets. New agreements and renewed contracts with OEMs tells us voice tech is becoming harder to ignore in cars. Personally, and I only bring this up because I'm not alone in this thought process, I'm more than happy driving my now vintage Dodge Ram that doesn't talk nor track my movements. But I digress.

  • Restaurant Orders (Q4): 9M+ calls.

Beyond the dashboard, better-connected systems gave restaurants a lift, with more orders coming in. At the same time, voice-based shopping started to show early signs of momentum, linking cars, televisions, and businesses together to make buying things easier and more seamless. Keep in mind, no single customer made up more than 10% of total revenue. It's not uncommon to come across smaller AI companies which often depend heavily on just one or two major clients, which can be risky. Fewer dependencies, more stability, that’s what spreading revenue across customers buys them, along with time.

  • Q4 GAAP Gross Margin: 48%
  • Q4 Non-GAAP Gross Margin: 61%.

Meanwhile, efficiency looks like it's also on the upswing. SoundHound's gross margins improved because it upgraded its infrastructure, reduced cloud costs, combined older systems, and intentionally moved away from lower-quality contracts. At the same time, the company still chose to invest in certain deals that have strong potential for automation in the future. Profits dip in the short run, but the company is betting on a longer-term return.

  • R&D Expense: $24.8M (+22% YoY)
  • Sales & Marketing Expense: $17.4M (+82% YoY)
  • G&A Expense: $21.2M (+29% YoY)
  • Adjusted EBITDA: -$7.4M (improved 56% YoY)
  • Cash & Equivalents: $248M
  • Debt: $0.

Still, operating costs went up, mainly because the company made acquisitions, which added more employees and strengthened its development capabilities. The company also increased its sales and marketing efforts. Even so, the adjusted EBITDA loss got smaller. The company also has no debt and a solid amount of cash, so it’s not under any immediate financial pressure.

  • Monthly Query Volume: billions/month (12x since IPO).

As I noted earlier, the tech looks highly replicable to me and open to disruption. Naturally, management sees it the other way, and pointed to the results as proof of what they say are their technical advantages. Their moat, if you will. They've built up decades of conversational data, use their own models that they claim are more accurate and cheaper than those from big tech companies, and rely on a system that combines set rules, generative AI, and human support.

As CEO Keyvan Mohajer put it,

“we believe we are entering a new era where companies with deep tech and data moats will create the most value,” adding that SoundHound “has a massive amount of data and has processed billions of interactions over the years across all major global languages,” which they argue gives them scale advantages that “new players are faced with” and “we’ve long since overcome.”

He said what separates them is owning the stack end to end and dialing it in, adding,

“we have our own models that we’ve created over a long period of time with a lot of data and we compare it with the big tech models and we beat them in accuracy, speed and cost,”

but not so rigid that they can’t pull in outside models when required.

At the platform level, he made the case that the advantage is structural: “our Agentic platform is the only solution that is ready to be deployed across a multitude of vertical use cases… all with a unified AI agent framework,” allowing customers to “build an agent once and deploy it anywhere,” and combining “agentic, deterministic, and human-assisted understanding” into a single system.

That said, it’s important to stay realistic. Voice AI is a very competitive space. Big tech companies provide the core technology, while many newer companies are competing for their share of the market. SoundHound does have an edge, though. Its deep expertise in specific industries like hospitality and automotive gives it an advantage that more general, one-size-fits-all competitors don’t have.

However, there are also some concerns. The company just announced that its CFO will leave in early April, and co-founder James Hom will step in as interim CFO. This creates a period of transition. Managing finances and deciding how to invest money are especially critical during times of rapid growth. Even if the change goes smoothly, it can still distract attention from other priorities.

SoundHound AI: Valuation on Full Volume

Speaking of priorities, let's look at SOUN's valuation. Clearly, investors are paying a massive premium compared to others in the same sector. The EV to sales ratio is 18.13x based on past performance and 13.15x based on expected future performance. In comparison, the typical company in the sector has ratios of 3.26 and 2.97.

The pattern repeats in other metrics. The price-to-sales ratio is 18.77 based on past results and 14.20 based on expected future results, compared to typical sector levels of 3.20 and 3.01. Meanwhile, the price-to-book ratio is 7.13, while the sector average is 3.55.

Cut through it, and the market's pricing in outsized growth. The smoking gun most likely comes from very strong growth numbers: revenue has increased by 99.45% year-over-year, and is expected to grow another 48.93% going forward. This is far higher than the usual sector growth rate of around 10%.

However, the company's still losing a lot of money, so usual profit measures don’t really tell us much.

It's also constantly spending more cash than it brings in. On top of that, the same old issues are still there. Losses, dilution, and a reliance on external funding haven’t gone away.

What you’re looking at here is the share count exploding over time, climbing from barely 12 million a few years ago to over 400 million today, which is the clearest sign that the company has been funding its growth by issuing more and more stock.

Final Verdict

Rating: Sell. The downside risk is much bigger than the potential upside. SOUN's trading at very high valuation levels while still losing money and burning through cash. The company's growing quickly, and that growth is real. The catch is the stock already reflects a best-case scenario playing out over years.

One final note, a possibility I’ve been seeing making the rounds, is that SOUN could absolutely become a more attractive takeover target if the share price falls further. I haven’t dug into the options chain yet, but at the right price, a strangle could make sense with that bet in mind.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SCHD: Winning The Rotation Trade With High-Quality Yield
data.description:
SCHD Still Looks Good For The Capital Rotation Trade

In my previous article on Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) that came out in mid-September 2025, I expected that the extreme tech valuations at the time would eventually lead to a capital rotation to dividend-paying stable stocks, like most of the names within SCHD's holdings. That was the reason for my core expectation of SCHD likely beating the S&P 500 index (SP500), at least in the short term. And so it happened - SCHD has beaten SPY since September 2025 as I expected:

I believe that the core structure of the fund - the combo of high-quality dividend payers and low expense ratio - makes SCHD a nice pick for the current macro environment when we're talking about capital preservation and risk-focused growth. I wouldn't expect massive, extraordinary returns for value/GARP investors from SCHD, but it's a stable ETF whose strategy keeps working as expected, and even major geopolitical risk-offs can't shake it as much as some of its peers. The capital rotation trade doesn't seem to be over (at least for now), so I keep my Buy on SCHD in place.

Why Do I Think So?

If you've read my most recent articles on big tech firms, you probably know that I'm bullish on Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), and most other mega-cap AI enablers because I believe most of them won't lose the moat that helped them increase their market cap and valuation over the past few decades. But even as a bull, I can't cancel out the fact that these companies spend their FCFs like crazy:

While I believe most of those FCFs will eventually translate into tangible returns, I agree that there's a risk that a) it can take a lot of time, and b) it may not be realized in full. If these risks look more likely to you than to me, and your risk aversion doesn't let you accept them, then buying SPY alone doesn't really make sense - the tech exposure there is still high despite the fact that the MAG 7 (MAGS) is down almost 9% YTD at the time of this writing.

Capital is now clearly rotating from mega caps that burn cash on AI initiatives to less capitalized companies and names that are unlikely to be disrupted much by AI, and are able to keep their FCF generation capacity in solid shape. This is where SCHD comes in with its exposure to Energy, where FCF generation benefits massively from the geopolitical crisis in the Middle East (and generally in the world). But even if oil prices crater tomorrow, the biggest sectoral exposure of SCHD at less than 22% (Energy exposure) wouldn't let it lose that much, in my opinion.

The top 10 holdings within SCHD's portfolio account for <43%, with the biggest 3 positions accounting for 4.82%, 4.32%, and 4.31%, respectively. The smallest top-10 position is 4.01% - it's PepsiCo's (PEP) ordinary shares. How does it compare to SPY? Well, SPY's top-10 accounts for only 36.35%, which looks better at first glance. But the biggest 3 positions account for 7.31%, 6.63%, and 4.96%, while the smallest on the top-10 list is only 1.57%. You see - the composition is clearly skewed, and if tech underperforms against energy or industrials, then SCHD is likely to keep beating SPY again and again in the next few months.

There's a catch here - you're buying SCHD for rising dividends, while SPY is used for a mix of dividends and growth (and, of course, the constituents' size). I've taken SCHD's top ten holdings and took what's important in this regard - the next year's yields and the n+1 growth in dividend payments (i.e., the 2028 growth in most cases). Also, I've added the Seeking Alpha Quant's Dividend Safety Score to see how the quantitative system views the companies' dividend safety (this metric basically removes my possible bias).

# Name Dividend Yield - 2027 Dividend growth in 2028 Dividend safety, SA Quant
1 Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) 2.28% 6.20% A+
2 ConocoPhillips (COP) 2.84% 2.85% A-
3 Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) 5.78% 1.74% C+
4 Chevron Corporation (CVX) 3.74% 4.57% B
5 Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) 4.38% 0.77% B-
6 Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) 3.04% 5.75% A-
7 Altria Group, Inc. (MO) 6.89% 4.01% B-
8 Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN) 3.12% 4.87% A
9 The Coca-Cola Company (KO) 2.94% 4.91% B-
10 PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) 4.02% 5.02% A-

Source: Seeking Alpha data, compiled by the author.

The average yield among the top ten is ~3.9%, while the payouts are projected to rise by over 4% (on average) in 2028. All this looks very good, in my opinion. If we take SCHD's peer like Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG), we'd see that a) its holdings structure favors tech, hence it's risky under the assumption of the ongoing capital rotation, and b) the yields are much lower as most of the companies prefer buybacks over dividends.

Another peer - iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) - looks better diversified as its top-10 accounts for 26.4% of the whole fund. But when we pick the constituents individually, we're not getting the same ~4% growth in 2028 dividends - DGRO's overdiversification lowers the dividend growth factor, which is why its TTM dividend yield is only 2.08% (SCHD's TTM dividend yield is 3.38%, which makes a difference when we assume millions of dollars invested).

In addition to that, SCHD's expense ratio of only 0.06% looks reasonable versus its peers, in my view, given the above forward dividend growth superiority.

The Bottom Line

As I wrote in my September article on SCHD, I agree that I may be wrong and premature in my judgments regarding the continuation of the current rotation cycle from aggressive assets like SPY - and especially NASDAQ 100 (QQQ) - to defensive ETFs like SCHD that can show more predictable total returns in today's market environment. There's a huge difference between today's market and what we saw in 1999 or 2008, when this kind of rotation was taking a lot of time, because today's fundamentals of the biggest companies within SPY seem to largely stomach the pain of the current CAPEX cycle in the next few years.

Despite the above risks to my bullish thesis on SCHD, I believe that this ETF still looks like a great place for capital reallocation in case the market decides to put the eggs into a safer basket. It's already happening - SCHD has beaten the SPY since calendar Q3 2025. But it looks like just the beginning because a) big tech is unlikely to stop its spending (hence, the risks of low-ROIC will stay elevated), and b) SCHD's dividend growth looks attractive if we assume the rate cuts are on the horizon. Again, I'm a believer that Amazon, Microsoft, and Google will survive the CAPEX cycle, and they'll be hugely profitable in the future. But SCHD looks like a good place to be at the moment if you're not willing to take the tech risks, in my opinion. I keep my Buy on SCHD in place.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Is There An ETF Alternative To Private Markets?
data.description:
Hybrid public-private exchange-traded funds (ETFs) aim to provide exposure to select private companies while leveraging the liquidity and transparency of public markets.

But what if investors aren’t actually realizing the upside they expect from those private companies, and/or are underperforming in the public portion of their portfolio because of how each component is structured?

If the private portion of a hybrid ETF isn’t structured effectively, investors may not obtain the exposure characteristics they expect from private market investments. It’s also important to remember that at least 85% of a hybrid ETF’s portfolio is typically invested in liquid public markets.1 When that portion is not constructed effectively, it can potentially affect the fund’s overall performance in unintended ways.

Avoiding Negative Surprises in Hybrid Portfolios

Some hybrid ETFs have struggled to deliver on expectations and have led some people to question the concept. But we believe the idea of combining public and private market exposure remains sound when executed thoughtfully.

If your goal is exposure to specific private companies rather than an opaque, pooled structure, it can be far more effective to participate directly on that company’s capitalization table rather than through a layered vehicle such as a special purpose vehicle (SPV). Direct exposure provides clearer ownership rights, potentially lower fees, and more transparent alignment with the underlying company’s performance.

In comparison, some hybrid structures may have limited transparency, added complexity, and additional costs that make it challenging for investors to clearly assess where value creation is occurring. Understanding how private exposure is structured and how it contributes to a portfolio is key when evaluating the role such investments are intended to play, particularly given their lower liquidity.

The Importance of the Public Side

Even for hybrid ETFs with private market allocations, the majority of the portfolio remains in publicly traded securities. This makes portfolio construction on the public side crucial. Chasing the “hot” private opportunities while neglecting the broader public equity exposure can lead to uneven outcomes.

That’s where the experience of a skilled fund manager or sub-advisor matters, one who can manage diversified* portfolios and apply risk management processes across different market environments.

Beyond Tech: The Broader Private Equity Lens

Some hybrid ETFs focus narrowly on sectors such as software, AI, or other innovation-driven themes. Yet private equity (PE) as an asset class provides exposure to far more than technology; it includes industrials, healthcare, consumer, and other sectors that provide diversification* and resilience across market cycles.

While some of the software and technology companies that have been selling off in late 2025 and early 2026 may eventually rebound, the recent sector volatility has highlighted the importance of avoiding overconcentration and building balanced exposure across industries.

KraneShares offers two ETFs that provide exposure to private-market investment characteristics and select private companies through liquid ETF structures:

Access Private Equity-Related Investment Drivers With BUYO

For investors seeking exposure to the characteristics of companies targeted by PE, rather than direct ownership of specific private companies, there are now liquid strategies available.

These approaches use quantitative models to identify and mimic the exposures that have historically been observed in private equity investing. KraneShares partnered with Man Group, the world's largest publicly traded hedge fund manager and an alternative investments specialist, to launch the KraneShares Man Buyout Beta Index ETF (BUYO).2 BUYO seeks to provide exposure to selected PE return drivers through a liquid, transparent ETF structure. This method can be thought of as a “smart beta” approach for PE, providing access to PE-like risk premia within a public market vehicle.

Just as ETFs exist to track broad asset classes like U.S. large-cap equities, emerging markets, or fixed income segments, investors can now access selected PE return drivers through a liquid ETF structure.

Access Select Private Companies With AGIX

For investors seeking direct ownership of select private innovators, including Anthropic and SpaceX, as well as a public equity sleeve with risk controls and index-based construction, KraneShares has a potential solution.

KraneShares partnered with Etna Capital Management, founded by AI-native engineers and investors that are embedded in today's AI ecosystem, to launch the KraneShares Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF (AGIX). Since its inception on 7/17/2024, AGIX has delivered notable outperformance relative to the Nasdaq 100 Index, which is a major technology and AI benchmark, returning +39.09% versus 27.54% over the same period.3 Additionally, AGIX's lower overlap with crowded mega-cap tech and focus on emerging AI companies across hardware, infrastructure, and applications is highlighted by having just a 50.20% holdings overlap with the Nasdaq-100 Index.4

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed or sold, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted.

By systematically targeting companies with Etna's proprietary AI Exposure Score, AGIX has identified AI-related opportunities that may be underrepresented in broad tech indices such as the Nasdaq-100 Index. Importantly, AGIX’s private market sleeve is designed to complement, not overshadow, its public equity core, which remains the majority of the portfolio.

Just as investors can now access characteristics of companies targeted by PE through BUYO — AGIX offers a differentiated approach to marrying public and private markets through a liquid, exchange-traded format.

Conclusion

Hybrid ETFs hold promise but also pitfalls, particularly when their private allocations are structured through SPVs or when the public side fails to complement the total portfolio exposure. We believe investors should remain mindful that these structures are complex and require disciplined design to deliver the intended exposure and portfolio characteristics.

BUYO seeks to offer a broad-based, fully liquid avenue that provides exposure to a subset of public equities that feature the key characteristics of companies historically held in PE/buyout funds. Alternatively, AGIX takes a more concentrated approach, blending public and private investments tied to the AI ecosystem. For investors seeking exposure to private market characteristics or select private companies within a liquid, publicly traded ETF wrapper, these strategies offer two distinct yet complementary approaches.

*Diversification does not ensure a profit or guarantee against a loss. AGIX and BUYO are non-diversified. The statement regarding diversification refers to sector exposure, not regulatory diversification under the Investment Company Act of 1940.

Citations:

  1. Regulatory Cap: SEC Rule 22e-4 restricts open-end funds and ETFs to holding a maximum of 15% of their net assets in illiquid investments. Target Assets: The 15% illiquid portion often targets private equity, venture capital, or private debt for higher growth potential.
  2. Data from "Managing the World’s Largest Publicly Traded Hedge Fund: Man Group CEO Robyn Grew," Goldman Sachs.
  3. Data from Bloomberg as of 2/28/2026.
  4. Data from Bloomberg as of 12/31/2025.

Index Definitions:

Nasdaq 100 Index: The Nasdaq 100 tracks the performance of 100 of the largest and most actively traded stocks on the Nasdaq exchange, with a technology sector focus.

Solactive Etna Artificial General Intelligence Index: The Solactive Etna Artificial General Intelligence Index is designed to capture the performance of global securities with a high business exposure to the AI business. This includes companies active in the provision of AI Hardware, AI Infrastructure and AI Applications.

Definitions:

Capitalization Table (Cap table): A record of a company’s owners and their ownership percentages, showing how equity is distributed among shareholders and investors.

Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV): A separate legal entity created for a specific investment purpose, often used to hold a single private investment or group of assets.

Quantitative Models: Data-driven mathematical and statistical strategies used to identify patterns or factors that influence investment performance.

Smart Beta: A rules-based investment approach designed to capture specific factors such as value, momentum, or quality rather than simply tracking a market index.

Beta: A measure of how an investment moves relative to the overall market, used to describe its risk and return characteristics.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Goldman Sachs International Equity Insights Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The MSCI EAFE Index returned 4.86% in the fourth quarter of 2025. This capped a strong year, ending at 31.22%. International stocks outperformed their US counterparts over the quarter and the year. Central banks around the world have taken different paths to cutting rates. The Fed's move toward easing, typically softening the US dollar and boosting overseas revenue translation, has been supportive for certain EAFE constituents. Overall, easing global financial conditions, a strong precious metals market, and geopolitical dynamics continued to drive international equities this past quarter. Additionally, value stocks outperformed growth stocks during the period.

Performance Review

In the fourth quarter of 2025, our International Equity Insights Fund returned 6.16%, outperforming its MSCI EAFE Index benchmark by 130 basis points over the period (net, I-share). All four of our investment pillars contributed to excess returns over the period, as well as country tilts.

Our Themes and Trends investment pillar contributed the most to our excess returns in the fourth quarter. Our suite of signals focusing on industry momentum and economic linkages guided overweight positioning in select diversified Metals & Mining companies, particularly within the UK, Sweden, and Australia. Such names performed strongly in the quarter due to their exposure to precious metals where demand rose amid global tension.

Our Fundamental Mispricings pillar was also additive to performance in the period. Our suite of signals focused on industry rotations guided overweight positioning within specific Diversified Bank companies that outperformed over the period due to strong earnings and resilient profits.

Our High-Quality Business Models pillar was also additive. Select underweights to European software companies contributed to excess returns. Some of these companies struggled amid geopolitical uncertainty as well as a challenging competitive landscape.

Finally, our Sentiment Analysis pillar contributed to performance. Losses stemming from overweight positioning within Aerospace and Defense companies were more than offset by our stock selection within Financials, notably Insurance and Capital Markets companies.

Country Tilts also contributed to performance across the period, although to a lesser extent than our pillars.

Summary Statistics

AUM (All Share Classes, $M) $5,011
Morningstar Category Foreign Large Blend
Inception Date Aug 15, 1997
Number of Holdings 369
Benchmark MSCI EAFE Index
Expense Ratios (Inst'l Share Class), Net/Gross 0.82% / 0.87%

Pillar Contributions To Excess Returns (Gross)

Performance reflects cumulative total returns for periods of less than one year and average annual total returns for periods of greater than one year. All Fund performance data reflect the reinvestment of distributions. Standardized Total Returns can be found on the following page. The expense ratios of the Fund, both current (net of any fee waivers or expense limitations) and before waivers (gross of any fee waivers or expense limitations) are as set forth above. Pursuant to a contractual arrangement, the Fund's waivers and/or expense limitations will remain in place through at least 2/28/26, and prior to such date the investment adviser may not terminate the arrangements without the approval of the Fund's Board of Trustees. Source: MSCI, Goldman Sachs Asset Management. Please refer to the Fund's prospectus for the most recent expenses.

The returns represent past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The Fund's investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted above. Please visit Financial Intermediaries to obtain the most recent month-end returns. The standardized total returns are average annual total returns or cumulative total returns (only if the performance period is one year or less) as of the most recent calendar quarter-end. They assume reinvestment of all distributions at net asset value. Because Institutional shares do not involve a sales charge, such a charge is not applied to their standardized total returns.

Past performance does not guarantee future results, which may vary. Fund holdings and allocations shown are unaudited, and may not be representative of current or future investments. Fund holdings and allocations may not include the Fund's entire investment portfolio, which may change at any time. Fund holdings should not be relied on in making investment decisions and should not be construed as research or investment advice regarding particular securities. Current and future holdings are subject to risk. Contribution to gross excess return refers to the impact of the factors listed above on the fund's gross performance. Attribution is relative to the MSCI EAFE Index. Source: MSCI

The Goldman Sachs International Equity Insights Fund invests primarily in a broadly diversified portfolio of equity investments in non-U.S. issuers. The Fund's investments are subject to market risk, which means that the value of the securities in which it invests may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies, particular sectors or governments and/or general economic conditions. Foreign and emerging markets investments may be more volatile and less liquid than investments in U.S. securities and are subject to the risks of currency fluctuations and adverse economic, social or political developments. The securities of mid- and small-capitalization companies involve greater risks than those associated with larger, more established companies and may be subject to more abrupt or erratic price movements. The Investment Adviser's use of quantitative models to execute the Fund's investment strategy may fail to produce the intended result. Different investment styles (e.g., "quantitative") tend to shift in and out of favor, and at times the Fund may underperform other funds that invest in similar asset classes. The Fund may have a high rate of portfolio turnover, which involves correspondingly greater expenses which must be borne by the Fund, and is also likely to result in short-term capital gains taxable to shareholders.

Past performance does not guarantee future results, which may vary. The value of investments and the income derived from investments will fluctuate and can go down as well as up. A loss of principal may occur

Fundamental Mispricings: We believe that buying high-quality businesses at a fair price leads to strong performance in the long-run

High Quality Business Models: We believe in companies generating high-quality revenues with sustainable business models and aligned management incentives

Market Themes and Trends: Global markets are increasingly theme and trend-driven. We believe that alternative data sources can provide us a lens into trends affecting companies globally, trends that other investors may not be seeing.

Sentiment Analysis: We believe that other market participants can provide valuable information to supplement our own analysis. By analyzing broader market sentiment, we believe we can gain insight into future stock performance.

The MSCI EAFE Index is designed to represent the performance of large and mid-cap securities across 21 developed markets, including countries in Europe, Australasia and the Far East, excluding the U.S. and Canada. The Index is unmanaged and the figures for the Index do not include any deduction for fees, expenses, or taxes. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index.

The MSCI EAFE Index is an equity index which captures large and mid cap representation across 21 Developed Markets countries* around the world, excluding the US and Canada. With 693 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each country.

This information discusses general market activity, industry or sector trends, or other broad-based economic, market or political conditions and should not be construed as research or investment advice. data.title: Bridgestone: War-Driven Selloff May Be Creating A Contrarian Buy Opportunity
data.description:
Automotive companies have seen significant share price pressure following the war in the Middle East. One company that has also seen significant pressure on its share price is Bridgestone (BRDCF, BRDCY). In this report, I discuss the company's earnings as well as the outlook, and those already show why the stock price slid 14% since the start of the month. However, I do believe that the valuation could offer a higher-risk, yet compelling, entry point.

Bridgestone Had A Tough Year Navigating Tariffs And Sluggish Demand

The results for 2025 show that the company booked no sales growth. Sales were flat at ¥4.4 trillion. That flat performance was a combination of strategic choices and macro pressures. On one hand, the company focused on premium tires rather than low-margin volume segments, and that already takes out some of the growth. That also aligned with the company's factory closures with exit from weaker markets. On the non-strategic side, the company has been dealing with the impact of sluggish global car production, weak demand in Latin America, and tariffs, which had a ¥25 billion impact. The focus on premium segments and cost rationalization also showed in the 2% growth in adjusted profits and 4% on a constant currency, leading to margins improving from 10.9% to 11.1% and a 40 basis point improvement on constant currency.

In the market for passenger car tires, sales increased 1%, but adjusted operating profit declined 5%, with margins contracting from 11.4% to 10.7%. Given that this segment generates around 56.5% of the company's revenue, low growth or margin erosion has a big impact on the overall business. The segment for bus and truck tires was flat at roughly ¥1 trillion, but profits improved from ¥57.9 billion to ¥88.4 billion. The specialty tire business covering heavy-duty vehicles, airplanes, agricultural equipment, and motorcycles was flat at ¥623.4 billion, with profits dropping 10% to ¥125.5 billion on a weaker business environment in the Americas. Diversified product sales were down 4% to ¥287.1 billion, but margins rose from 4.2% to 11.3%.

In Japan, sales rose 3% and profit increased 6%, while in Asia, sales declined 2%, but profits increased 2%. In the Americas, sales declined 2%, but profit increased 12%, and in EMEA, sales rose 2% and profits increased 42%. So, the company's performance is not necessarily impressive when looking at the growth rates, but in geographical markets and end markets by tire class, the company navigated different dynamics reasonably well.

This Year Could Become Another Challenging Year

For 2026, the company expects 2% growth carried by truck and bus tires as well as replacement tires. The focus on premium tires and cost reduction is expected to drive a 4% increase in profits with margins up 30 basis points. The company expects raw material costs to decline with price, mix, and volume all contributing positively, showing the positive impact of the cost rationalization and focus on higher margin business first before focusing on volume.

The difficult part for Bridgestone is that the macro outlook already is not great, and its guidance shows only small improvement in revenues and a bigger improvement in profits, but it is still relatively small. The situation in the Middle East could erode the growth expectations for the year. Essentially, Bridgestone is a levered play on global mobility. Higher oil prices may deflate the macro growth picture while also pressuring consumer confidence, reducing car purchases and tire replacements. Pressure on global car manufacturing already gave the company a very difficult execution landscape last year.

The spike in oil prices also has an impact on raw material costs. Tires are not just rubber or one sort of rubber. There are various rubbers.

  1. Natural rubber
  2. Synthetic rubbers
    1. Styrene-butadiene rubber
    2. Butadiene rubber
    3. Nitrile Butadiene rubber

Furthermore, there is carbon black to provide strength and durability. The synthetic rubbers and carbon black are derived from petrochemical processes. So, they are oil-linked. Furthermore, logistics costs for raw materials and finished goods also increase as oil prices head higher. So, Bridgestone is likely to see lower demand, higher input costs, and higher logistics costs, and potentially inflation may also come into play.

Bridgestone Could Be a Contrarian Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Bridgestone, I implemented a 4% pressure against prior sales estimates for 2026 and 2027, as well as an 8% pressure on EBITDA due to the loss of leverage and 12% on free cash flow. I do believe that Bridgestone is somewhat more shielded compared to carmakers due to tire replacement needs. Currently, I am not assuming the aforementioned pressures are on a year-on-year basis but a reduction against the in-year estimates. So, if we are looking at year-on-year declines, then the buy case would be shakier.

However, with the pressures implemented, the company would basically see little growth and rely mostly on cost optimization driving 8.5% annual EBITDA growth, while free cash flow was already set to decline as capital expenditures are expected to increase significantly. So, I am marking this a contrarian buy with a $20.34 price target, indicating 10% upside.

While the situation in the Middle East could bring a drag, we note that ongoing cost optimization should still result in significantly higher EBITDA margins compared to 2026. Free cash flow is expected to decline, driven by higher capital intensity, as CapEx as a percentage of sales will grow from 6% to 9%. I also believe that the company does offer a reasonable yield, and it has little debt on the balance sheet relative to its cash balance. This could also allow the company to repurchase shares in times of a weak market environment, which in the process will help the company's dividend sustainability.

Conclusion: A Tough Year Ahead, But Bridgestone Has Some Appeal

I do believe that, given the higher input costs and the uncertain macroeconomic outlook, there is some risk for cyclical companies such as Bridgestone. However, I also believe that the company's cost optimizations can still outshine macroeconomic headwinds. As a result, I am marking the stock a contrarian buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2 Reliable And Undervalued Dividends For Passive Income
data.description:
Investors have a lot on their plates with the current market turbulence. For one thing, 'SaaS-pocalypse' has investors worried about AI disruption to software companies. Another factor comes from turbulence in the Middle East, as that has rocked energy markets. As such, investors may take comfort in parking capital into hard assets that provide durable income streams.

This brings me to the following 2 picks, both of which offer investors solid income from infrastructure and real assets. Both are trading at attractive valuations well below historical norms. Let's explore what makes each a resounding 'Buy' for income and capital appreciation potential!

#1: Verizon

Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) is one of the Big 3 telcos in the U.S., alongside peers AT&T Inc. (T) and T-Mobile US, Inc. (TMUS). It offers wireless services, broadband connectivity, and enterprise solutions to consumers, businesses, and government customers. VZ's network infrastructure spans mobility and fiber, positioning it at the center of growing demand for high-speed connectivity and data-driven services.

In recent months, Verizon has entered a period of strategic transition with a CEO change. This has put a renewed focus on operational efficiency, customer experience, and shareholder value.

This focus is starting to show in the numbers, as VZ closed out 2025 with strong Q4 momentum. This included more than 1 million net additions across mobility and broadband, VZ's best quarterly volume in six years. Postpaid phone net additions reached 616K, including 551K from the consumer segment.

Broadband also provided meaningful contributions with 372K net adds driven by FWA (fixed wireless access). Notably, VZ's fiber performance was also solid, with Fios adding 67K subscribers and recently acquired Frontier contributing 125K fiber net additions. As shown below, FWA saw a ramp-up in net adds during Q4 after 3 consecutive quarters of declining net additions.

These results meant that VZ didn't have to lean on price growth to drive financial results. As shown below, total wireless service and total company revenue grew by 2% and 2.5% YoY for the full year, respectively. It's worth noting that this only includes one quarter's worth (Q4) of the new CEO being in place.

Not having to lean on price growth to drive revenues means that VZ can focus more on driving customer value, retention, and sustainable volume expansion. This represents a shift away from prior pricing actions, which resulted in elevated churn in a competitive environment.

At the same time, VZ is saving costs, with a target of $5 billion in operating expense reductions in 2026 alone. This could continue to drive higher free cash flow. In 2025, VZ generated $20 billion in FCF after accounting for $37 billion in operating income and $17 billion in capital expenditures.

Looking ahead, VZ could be in the early stages of a turnaround, as management expects to generate between 750K and 1 million in postpaid net additions this year. This could drive bottom line growth, as management is guiding for 4-5% adjusted EPS growth for the full year at the midpoint of the range.

At the same time, fiber is central to VZ's strategy with the addition of Frontier's assets. Management expects to expand its fiber footprint, and the existing footprint could enable wireless penetration through cross-selling. Plus, VZ now expects to generate $1 billion in run-rate operating cost synergies by 2028, which is double initial estimates.

VZ maintains a BBB+ credit rating from S&P with the support of a strong balance sheet. It had a safe net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 2.2x before the Frontier acquisition. Management expects to return to the 2.0x to 2.25x range by 2027 through debt reduction and strong cash flow generation.

Importantly for income investors, VZ raised its dividend by 2.5% this year, marking its 20th consecutive year of dividend growth. The dividend is well covered by a 58% payout ratio. VZ also has a $25 billion share buyback program in place that's to be executed over 3 years.

VZ is no longer dirt cheap at the current price of $49.59 with a forward P/E of 10.1. However, it remains undervalued compared to its historical valuation of 13.0, as shown below.

At the current valuation, I believe the market has yet to warm up to VZ's renewed growth story and strategy. This is especially as VZ is poised to benefit from increased AI adoption through the associated connectivity needs. Analysts estimate 7-9% EPS growth annually over the next two years. Combined with the 5.7% dividend yield, this could drive double-digit total returns from here.

#2: Healthpeak Properties

Healthpeak Properties, Inc. (DOC), is a large, diversified healthcare REIT focused on owning and operating high-quality assets across outpatient medical facilities, life science lab properties, and senior housing communities.

It has spent the past several years reshaping its portfolio. This includes its merger with Physicians Realty Trust (outpatient medical) and a strategic pullback from life science development at the peak of that cycle. These earlier decisions are now proving critical. They have allowed DOC to stabilize earnings through one of the most challenging environments in the life science sector due to overbuilding.

At present, DOC has 689 properties across major U.S. markets. Its largest tenant relationship is with HCA Healthcare, Inc. (HCA), a well-regarded medical system. Besides CommonSpirit Health, no other tenant represents more than 1% of ABR, as shown below.

DOC demonstrated solid results during Q4 2025, with same-store cash NOI growing by 3.9% YoY. This is consistent with the full year's results, with SSNOI growth of 4%. These results were achieved against a backdrop of declining occupancy in life science assets. This was more than offset by growth in senior housing and outpatient medical, which saw SSNOI growth of 17% and 4% YoY, respectively.

Looking ahead, pressures on the life science space may begin to ease. The leasing pipeline for this segment has strengthened to 1.5 million square feet, representing a 50% YoY increase. Additionally, new supply is expected to drop near zero for the next several years. This includes the repurposing of lab space, which will help rebalance supply-demand dynamics in this sector.

One of the biggest news items for DOC as of late is its planned IPO of Janus Living, which will house DOC's fast-growing senior living assets. DOC's shareholders will not receive shares of this entity, as DOC plans to retain 85% ownership of Janus Living. I view this as a win for shareholders, as JL should be able to fetch a higher valuation multiple compared to DOC. This means that JL will be able to raise equity in an accretive manner and help accelerate growth for this segment.

Like VZ, DOC also maintains a strong balance sheet with a BBB+ credit rating from S&P. This is supported by a safe net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5.2x and $2.4 billion of liquidity. DOC currently yields 7%, and the dividend is well-covered by a 72% payout ratio.

I see value in DOC at the current price of $17.39 with a forward P/FFO of 10.0, sitting far below its historical P/FFO of 14.0, as shown below.

At the current valuation, I don't think the market is giving DOC sufficient credit for its solid performance. Plus, it appears to be ignoring DOC's catalysts from the higher growth potential in Janus Living. With a 7% dividend yield and my expectations for 5-7% annual FFO/share growth over the medium term, DOC could deliver double-digit total returns from here.

Risks to Consider

Risks to VZ include competitive dynamics in the telco industry, which could mean price wars that pressure margins. VZ also leans on cost savings to help drive growth. Too aggressive cost reductions could result in operational missteps. VZ also has capital intensity risk from its buildout of fiber assets.

Risks to DOC include its Life Science segment, which faces pressure from near-term oversupply. This could mean unexpected property downtime should tenants move out and not re-lease space. Plus, DOC has execution risks as it relates to the IPO of Janus Living, which is dependent on favorable equity prices to have a strong start.

Investor Takeaway

Both Verizon and Healthpeak Properties stand out as income investments anchored in durable assets. They offer investors a measure of stability amid market uncertainty.

Verizon is leveraging its core telecom infrastructure to drive a potential turnaround through subscriber growth, fiber expansion, and cost efficiencies. These elements support steady cash flow and a well-covered, growing dividend.

Meanwhile, Healthpeak is showing strength in outpatient and senior housing segments, which are offsetting temporary life science weakness. Its planned Janus Living IPO adds a long-term growth catalyst.

Despite operating in very different sectors, both VZ and DOC share key traits of strong BBB+ credit ratings, attractive yields, and improving operational momentum. Their historical valuations position them as compelling options for investors seeking a blend of income and capital appreciation potential.

data.title: Bank Of Japan Holds Rates And Offers No Signal About Its Next Move
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

The BoJ voted 8-1 to maintain the policy rate at 0.75%

As widely expected, the Bank of Japan kept its policy unchanged, with one dissenting vote from Takata Hajime, who favoured a 25bp hike. The BoJ’s analysis on economic conditions was tilted toward optimism as the BoJ expects the economy to continue growing moderately and inflation to rebound after a temporary deceleration.

Despite previous rate hikes and US tariffs over the past year, financial conditions remain accommodative, and the positive cycle between wages and inflation has continued. The BoJ acknowledged the risks surrounding the Middle East situation, but its outlook on the economy hasn’t differed much from the previous meeting. We think this analysis sets the stage for potential rate hikes in the months ahead.

No clarity over near-term policy decision

As we expected, Governor Kazuo Ueda did not drop any hint on the timing of the next rate hike at the press conference, as the BoJ would like to remain flexible amid the Middle East war. He repeated the directional guidance that if the economy continues to recover and inflation approaches to 2% in line with the BoJ’s projection, then the BoJ will continue to adjust monetary policy accordingly.

But his remarks throughout the press conference were extremely cautious. He mentioned that the BoJ would make an appropriate policy judgement while examining available data at each meeting, and underlying inflation could move in either direction. He mentioned that a slim majority of board members see more upside risks to inflation, but we don't think this means that the board's stance has shifted toward being more hawkish. Meanwhile, he was much clearer about other prerequisites for rate hikes: robust spring wage negotiations alongside a virtuous cycle of wage growth and increased consumer spending. This time, he added that the BoJ would keenly look for strong wage gains for small businesses.

Regarding the inflation outlook, he promised that the BoJ would communicate with markets on price developments. Thus, we will closely monitor the BoJ's communication going forward. This may be a better indicator to gauge the timing of the next rate hike.

BoJ watch

Summing up today's meeting statement and Governor Ueda's comments, we continue to believe that the BoJ will need more time to assess the impact of the recent supply shock-driven price gains and how this may change the underlying inflation trend. Meanwhile, government measures are likely to help alleviate some price burden.

The government's verbal interventions also appear to be keeping USDJPY below 160 levels for now. This will buy more time for the BoJ to be patient about the next rate hike. It is a close call, but we believe that the BoJ will make its next move in June.

Macro outlook updates

As we have revisited our base-case scenario for the Middle East development, we have adjusted the key macro outlook for Japan too.

We anticipate that a month-long closure of the Strait of Hormuz could impact growth primarily in the second quarter of 2026, followed by a steady recovery in the second half of the year. Activity data from January and February support our projection of continued GDP growth in 1Q26, building on the recovery seen in 4Q25. We have trimmed down 2Q26 and 3Q26 GDP, and the estimated 2026 GDP growth was lowered from 1.0% year-over-year to 0.8%.

The biggest decline comes from the widening of the trade deficit, while the impact on domestic demand should be limited thanks to various government measures. The burden of higher energy prices will be partly absorbed by government subsidies, while wages are expected to rise modestly. Thus, private consumption is likely to continue growing. As robust global AI investment continues amid heightened geopolitical tensions, defence and semiconductor-related investments are expected to grow as well. With the release of oil reserves and an increase in non-Middle East energy imports, interruption of industrial activity should be limited and temporary.

For inflation, we believe the upside pressures will accumulate and spread throughout the year. Under our new oil forecasts, we have added 0.2ppt of CPI to the annual average so that we now see 2.2% YoY growth from 2026. We believe that fuel price rises will be limited thanks to the price cap, and energy prices will also remain stable thanks to government subsidies. However, the second-round impact and weak currency will continue to add to price pressures until 2H26.

Headline inflation is expected to stay below 2% due to base effects and energy subsidies; however, it is expected to rebound above 2% the rest of the year. Core inflation excluding fresh food prices is also expected to stay below 2% but see a gradual pick-up to above 2% in 2H26.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Williams-Sonoma: Dividend Bump, Rising Comps, And Healthy Margins
data.description:
There is undoubtedly a lot of fear in the stock market right now, dominated by macro threats such as an extended conflict in the Middle East, higher-than-expected inflation, and the potential for a shaky macroeconomy to be further battered by AI. That said, as we wrap up the Q4 earnings season, a lot of the earnings data coming out of companies across sectors seems to suggest a much healthier landscape than the market's recent drop might suggest.

Williams-Sonoma (WSM), the furniture and home goods company that is also the parent company of Pottery Barn, is one such company. Despite fears about weaker consumer spending during the holidays, Williams-Sonoma delivered terrific Q4 comps and is looking ahead to further growth in FY26. Williams-Sonoma has been a relative outperformer in the market this year, with its stock roughly flat since the start of January and up nearly 10% over the past year. In my view, this outperformance is set to continue on the back of strong sales.

I last wrote a buy article on Williams-Sonoma in December, when the stock was trading at $190 per share. Now slightly lower amid stronger results, I continue to see a compelling entry point in this often-overlooked growth stock. I'm reiterating my buy rating here.

Well Positioned For Success In 2026: Potential Tariff Removal, Housing Market, Dividend Increase, And Store Portfolio Optimization

Beyond the strength of Williams-Sonoma's recent results, there are a couple of factors that drive my enthusiasm for this name. The first is the fact that, needless to say, heavy tariffs had hung over Williams-Sonoma and the rest of its peers in the furniture industry. And while the Supreme Court only struck down the country-specific "reciprocal tariffs" and not the boosted tariff rates on upholstered furniture and kitchen cabinetry, we note that actual imposed tariff rates now vary by country, with the heavy 25% rate reserved for Chinese imports and most other imports, like those from the EU or Japan, facing lower tariff rates. The administration also pushed back the planned boost in the tariff rate to 50% (from the current 25% base rate) to 2027. In my view, this leaves a lot of time to renegotiate industry-specific tariff rates, especially as the reciprocal tariffs themselves are being challenged. In any case, Williams-Sonoma has managed to preserve its gross margin profile despite tariffs, indicating very strong cost management that not all of its rivals were able to do.

Another macro factor that we should consider is a potential rebound in the housing market. Mortgage rates continue to fall and recently broke below the 6% threshold. Many sidelined homebuyers may be waking up to the fact that COVID-era, low single-digit rates may never come back and wade back into the housing market, which would trigger additional demand for furniture.

The third factor is the fact that Williams-Sonoma remains focused on efficiency. It's closing down underperforming locations and driving its revenue growth via comp sales growth and e-commerce. In Q4, the company closed a further 10 locations across Pottery Barn, West Elm, and Williams-Sonoma, while opening 3 new locations. On a y/y basis, the company shrunk its store portfolio by -1%. We think this is the right move for the company to preserve its margins and focus on online sales as rent and labor increase.

Lastly, management's confidence in the business is well reflected in the company's recent quarterly dividend boost to $0.76, a meaningful 15% increase from its prior dividend rate. On an annualized basis, this translates to $3.04 in yield, or ~1.7% at current share prices. While Williams-Sonoma certainly isn't a dividend stock, the current dividend is still a reasonable inducement to hold the stock as we wait for a potential multiples re-rating.

And considering the company's strong guidance for FY26 (the year ending January 2027), which calls for comps continuing to grow between 2-6% and hitting a 17.5-18.1% operating margin (flat at the high end of the range, despite modeling a full-year hit from tariffs versus only a partial year in FY25, and before the heightened furniture tariffs took impact), we think the stock trades at a very reasonable valuation. Wall Street is expecting $9.20 in pro forma EPS this year, which puts Williams-Sonoma's valuation at 20x P/E: not a bargain stock, but considering its stronger position in the retail/furniture industry against many peers, and the positive comps trends despite a broadly weaker consumer macroeconomy, I consider this a great price to pay for a high-quality business.

Q4 Recap

Let's now go through Williams-Sonoma's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Revenue declined -4% y/y to $2.36 billion. However, we note that the company faced a very tough comparison against a 14-week fiscal quarter in Q4 of FY25; and on top of this, as previously mentioned, the company has been active in shrinking its store portfolio, which reduced by -1% y/y to 506 open stores as of the end of Q4.

The best way to assess Williams-Sonoma's growth, meanwhile, is its comp sales trends, which, as a reminder, measure the revenue growth from existing store locations without considering the contribution of newly opened (or recently closed) stores. In Q4, Williams-Sonoma's comps grew 3.2% y/y, which decelerated 80bps versus 4.0% growth in Q3.

We note particular strength in the West Elm and eponymous Williams-Sonoma brands, which grew 4.8% y/y and 7.2% y/y, respectively. We're newly cautious about weakness in the Pottery Barn business, which is the company's largest brand at roughly one-third of sales, and where comps decelerated from 1.3% growth in Q3.

The company highlighted success in hosting in-store events as critical to driving comp sales growth, particularly for the Williams-Sonoma brand, which is celebrating its 70th anniversary in 2026: going the company an opportunity for further marketing buzz. Per CEO Laura Alber's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Williams-Sonoma finished 2025 strong, with a positive 7.2% comp in Q4 and a positive 6.9% comp for the year. The Williams-Sonoma brand continued to outperform across the board. 2026 marks our 70th anniversary. And at 70 years, this brand is not mature. It is gaining momentum. The core of our kitchen business is accelerating, and our pipeline of proprietary in-house design products and market exclusives continue to separate us from the competition.

In Q4, customers came to us for holiday gifting, cooking and entertaining. Also, Williams-Sonoma benefited from a strong gift assortment with products that perform, design that is distinctive, and assortments that reflect both who our customer is and who they aspire to be. 2025 was our biggest year ever for in-store events at Sonoma. We held skill series, classes on Sundays, and we hosted 120 celebrity chef and influencer book signings. Highlights from the events in Q4 included signings with Martha Stewart, Trisha Yearwood, and Wishbone Kitchen. We look forward to welcoming customers into our stores throughout 2026 with even more opportunities to learn, engage and be inspired."

The company notes as well that B2B sales remain another bright spot. The company completed high-profile orders, including the Waldorf Astoria hotel in Beverly Hills. B2B sales grew 14% y/y in the fourth quarter and 10% y/y for the full year FY25, and the company notes it has a healthy pipeline entering FY26.

On the margin front, we respect the fact that Williams-Sonoma maintained its gross margins at 46.9%, falling only -40bps y/y despite the added burden of tariffs. The company notes that supply chain economies of scale helped to offset tariff headwinds (which drove a -170bps reduction to merchandising margins), as well as -80bps of deleverage on higher occupancy costs. The company also did a year-end inventory reconciliation and true-up and found that shrinkage fell below expectations, causing a favorable true-up to bridge the difference.

Operating margins fell -120bps y/y primarily due to higher sales and marketing expenses to boost core brands, but we note as well that the 14-week prior-year quarter also caused a tough operational comparison. Pro forma EPS of $3.04 fell -7% y/y (again, with a tough 14-week compare; but full-year EPS of $8.84 grew 1% y/y) but beat Wall Street's in-quarter expectations of $2.91 by a 5% spread.

Risks And Key Takeaways

Of course, there are still a number of risks that we need to keep in mind here. The tariff landscape remains highly uncertain, and though Williams-Sonoma has baked conservative assumptions into its forecast (new tariffs to replace the expiring tariffs midyear in 2026 and no tariff refunds), we could still see the Trump administration prevail in pushing forward reciprocal tariffs and heightened duties on furniture products. Also, the global macro could continue to deteriorate, hampering sales in key brands like Pottery Barn, which is already seeing slight declines.

That said, I think these risks are counterbalanced by the positives: impressive comps growth at the Williams-Sonoma and West Elm brands, strong margin maintenance in the wake of tariffs, and a boosted dividend. Stay long here.

data.title: Exzeo: Under The Radar And Gleaming
data.description:
I'll be the first one to say that I am not a technology guy. That said, sometimes, as was the case with Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS), the technology part is easy enough to understand, and the value proposition is so obvious, as is the valuation mispricing, that even a value guy like me can lean in. Exzeo Group, Inc. (XZO) is just such a case.

XZO is 82% owned by HCI Group, Inc. (HCI), a Florida-based insurer that deals mostly with property and casualty markets (I'll touch on HCI below). HCI IPO'd 18% of XZO in November at $21. Florida is a treacherous place to write P&C insurance, given the weather. HCI has been unusually successful because it has relied heavily on technology to underwrite its policies. That technology is XZO.

Whereas most insurers judge a home or property's risk by such things as flood plains, which are large areas on a map, XZO allows for specific property risk (e.g., the property might be in a flood plain, but it happens to be on a slight rise that keeps it from flooding). This property-specific analysis is critical. It allows the company to underwrite policies where other insurers don't and/or offer lower premiums than the competition. On the flip side, it helps the company avoid some losses that other insurers with less detailed information incur.

At the time of the IPO, HCI was XZO's only customer, but it had been breaking out numbers as a separate entity for some time, and intracompany pricing is market-based. Q3 revenue was $56 million, and EBITDA was $29 million.

Fourth Quarter Numbers

Q4 was the first full quarter reported as a standalone public company. It was a blowout. Revenue came in at $53.3 million, up from $44.5 million, and EPS was $0.25, up from $0.15. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $28 million, up from $16.8 million, with margins growing to 55% from 46%.

These numbers brought full-year results to $217 million in revenue, EPS to $0.99, and adjusted EBITDA to $111.5 million. With $305 million of cash, no debt, and only $70 million of current contract liabilities, 2025 results leave this company (at $15.88/share) trading at less than 16x earnings and about 10x EBITDA. These are mid-range to low multiples for almost any high-margin business, never mind one that is expanding those margins through double-digit revenue growth.

I believe XZO has been thrown in the bucket of software companies that have been beaten up lately due to perceived AI disintermediation risks. I believe this fear is severely misplaced. Not only does AI allow XZO to automate underwriting, thereby making the tool even more cost-efficient for insurer customers, but it also works on proprietary behind-the-firewall data sets that XZO built over the past 12 years. In essence, XZO has populated its own LLM.

If anything, the company believes AI will be a major net benefit for the company. They commented in the Q4 conference call:

Next, I want to take a few minutes to give our view on the approaching inflection point of AI in the insurance industry and why we think it will be positive for Exzeo. Much of the insurance industry still relies on human underwriters to manually review and touch nearly every policy. In an AI-driven world, that is going to change. Friction will come out of the system. We saw years ago that the industry would eventually be moving towards a fully automated insurance platform, where policies are bound in minutes and administered with little to no human intervention, which is what Exzeo currently does today.

The product's value is proven by the new partners the company has added since going public. One third party was already signed up as a customer by Q3 earnings, and another one signed up during Q4. The company expects these two companies alone to reach $100 million of managed premium this Q1. A potentially much larger customer signing was Tokio Marine Highland. XZO will distribute Tokio Marine's flood insurance in 42 states. This proves the company's technology works outside Florida and gives it the potential to scale its managed premium.

Guidance

Guidance sounds incredibly conservative. The company guided for $25 million of pretax income for Q1 and $120 million for 2025. That's only 9% growth, while managed premium is expected to grow to $1.55 billion, 12% growth. These numbers for Q1 are below Q4 numbers and below the annualized run rate, respectively. Unless momentum changes dramatically from XZO's business with HCI, which seems unlikely given HCI's results, and none of the third-party business catches on, I think the company will exceed these results.

Valuation

Valuation is very compelling in my opinion, given the growth prospects, high EBITDA margins, and high cash conversion. XZO has been caught up in the software maelstrom of the past few weeks. This product is fairly AI-proof in my opinion, as it is a specialized analysis, although AI is a risk in everything these days, I guess.

Market Cap (@$15.88) $1.44 billion
Cash $305 million
Enterprise Value

$1.135 billion
EV/2026 EBITDA (using $123) 9.5x
FCF yield (using $90 million) 8%

I believe that HCI will spin out its stake in XZO sometime in 2027. Until then, it's a small-cap without a huge amount of analyst coverage that might struggle for attention. That said, it's already at a discount to its IPO price (on a market convulsion), and the news stream should be very positive.

If you wanted a discounted way to play XZO, you could just buy HCI. Analysts value the insurance business at $160/share. That implies the XZO stake is worth almost $1.2 billion for free. The main issue is that some people (technology investors) just want to own XZO and don't want any piece of an insurer, so XZO can rocket higher without HCI moving or worse.

Risk

I think the main risks here are whether the company can continue to attract third-party customers and how much of a threat AI is. Hopefully, we see continued third-party signups, and the company can communicate how AI is not a threat to its franchise. Valuation, in my opinion, already discounts quite a bit of skepticism.

Conclusion

This is a little hidden jewel of a business. The next few years can be very exciting.

data.title: AtaiBeckley: BPL-003 Phase 3 Momentum Keeps Me Bullish
data.description:
AtaiBeckley Inc. (ATAI) is a clinical-stage company that develops psychedelic therapies for treatment-resistant depression (TRD) and social anxiety disorder (SAD). It was formed after a combination of Atai Life Sciences and Beckley Psytech. ATAI's pipeline includes BPL-003 for TRD, which will enter Phase 3 in Q2 2026 based on the efficacy shown in its recent Phase 2 trials. They found that this antidepressant's effect was rapid and lasted for weeks. ATAI's pipeline also includes VLS-01 for TRD in Phase 2 and EMP-01 for SAD in Phase 2a. Hence, I believe their R&D progress and healthy runway justify a reiterated “Buy” at these levels for long-term investors willing to speculate on therapeutic psychedelics.

Psychedelic Bet

AtaiBeckley Inc. is a clinical-stage biotech company developing mental health treatments, especially psychedelic-inspired therapies for serious psychiatric conditions. It was founded in 2018 as Atai Life Sciences. The company then changed its name to Atai Beckley N.V in November 2025 after merging with Beckley Psytech. But they later renamed the company again to AtaiBeckley Inc. in December 2025 after it redomiciled from the Netherlands to Delaware. ATAI is now headquartered in New York, NY. I also previously covered ATAI last October, but since then, the stock has declined by about 46.9%, so I thought it was worthwhile updating my thesis on this name.

At its core, I believe that ATAI's thesis is that existing treatments may help with symptoms of depression and anxiety. However, the current standard of care often requires frequent dosing over long periods and doesn't lead to lasting benefits. Fortunately, ATAI's drug candidates aim to solve this issue. They are psychedelic-based compounds that may offer a rapid onset, more durable effects, need less frequent dosing, and promote neuroplasticity.

Additionally, ATAI argues that psychedelic therapies create benefits through two connected layers at the same time. These two key therapeutic components are biological at the brain-level layer, which are coupled with a positive subjective psychological-experience layer. These two layers may reinforce each other and lead to durable symptom improvement. In the brain-level layer, ATAI suggests that its therapies could support brain repair, growth, and even strengthen connections between neurons. In fact, it's possible that ATAI's psychedelics could even change the way neurons communicate between brain regions.

Therefore, if ATAI's bet on psychedelics holds, the potential effect is that the brain becomes more flexible and better integrated. This is key, as it should help solve patients' dysfunctional patterns. And while psychedelics help, ATAI also focuses on the psychological-experience layer. ATAI's belief is that the intense experience of using these psychedelics may be therapeutic by itself. The experience includes an altered state of consciousness, where patients undergo a perceptual shift of themselves and the world. This includes a holistic cognitive reappraisal, where it's possible to reframe painful memories. This way, patients can ultimately reach emotional release, being able to process or express emotions previously suppressed.

ATAI's Clinical Pipeline

With that in mind, ATAI's pipeline currently includes the candidate BPL-003 for treatment-resistant depression (TRD). It's also worth mentioning that BPL-003 obtained FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation. Technically speaking, this is a mebufotenin benzoate nasal spray, or a formulation of a psychedelic-based 5-MeO-DMT candidate. The company completed BPL-003's End-of-Phase 2 (EOP 2) interaction with the FDA in Q1 2026 and plans to start Phase 3 in Q2 2026. We also should get its topline data readout by early 2029.

Moreover, ATAI recently reported that its Phase 2 trials showed that one dose of BPL-003 in a cohort of 12 patients led to encouraging results. These psychological benefits are measured according to the Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS). Essentially, the average score started at 27.5, which is consistent with serious depression, and fell to 14.8 by day 2 (equivalent to an improvement of 12.6 points). Notably, the improvement seemed sustainable at least until day 85 of the trial. And the candidate demonstrated a positive safety profile with no major side effects that could derail its R&D progress.

Aside from BPL-003, ATAI's pipeline also has VLS-01 for TRD. This candidate is in Phase 2, with topline Phase 2 data expected by 2H2026. VLS-01 is DMT delivered through buccal film. It can produce very rapid and intense altered states of consciousness, with a shorter duration, which could make it more practical in a clinical setting. Similarly, EMP-01 is another candidate. EMP-01 is in Phase 2a for social anxiety disorder (SAD), and ATAI reported its topline data in February 2026.

The trial showed promising efficacy results, as the difference between the EMP-01 vs. placebo groups was a reduction of 11.85 points on the Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale (LSAS). This was measured for patients using EMP-01 at day 43. Furthermore, using the Clinician Global Impression-Improvement (CGI-I) scale, 49% of EMP-01 patients were rated as “very much improved” or “much improved,” versus 15% on placebo. So, it's clear that ATAI's pipeline has several interesting candidates that are progressing and heading into late-stage trials in the near term.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, ATAI currently trades at a $1.3 billion market cap. Its latest 10-K shows its balance sheet holds $85.3 million in cash and $135.4 million in short-term securities. This amounts to approximately $220.7 million in liquid funds against no financial debt (aside from regular operating liabilities). As such, I estimate ATAI's EV at roughly $1.1 billion, and its book value reached $222.0 million. This does price ATAI at a relatively expensive 5.9 P/B, which is considerably higher than its peers' median P/B of 2.7.

And unfortunately, ATAI still doesn't have any approved products, and its revenues for FY2025 came only from licensing and R&D services (about $4.1 million). So, at this point, the bull case rests entirely on ATAI's ability to actually obtain approval for any of its pipeline candidates. In particular, I think the main value driver is probably BPL-003, as the recent trial data suggests it could have a best-in-class profile for TRD.

Additionally, TRD's TAM is projected to reach $4.8 billion by 2035, so it's undoubtedly a sizeable market opportunity for BPL-003. And, if ATAI taps into this TAM with a potentially best-in-class IP, it should position the company very well for a healthy recurrent revenue vertical. Plus, this asset is now heading into Phase 3 by 2H2026, with the company reaffirming it has an ample cash runway at least through 2029's topline readouts from both parallel Phase 3 trials on BPL-003.

Having said that, I see two main risks with ATAI at this point. First, this is still undoubtedly a long-term bull case, as BPL-003 is the leading candidate and its Phase 3 data readouts remain years away. Meanwhile, the company will likely keep burning through its cash reserves, and management may issue more stock to help fund its R&D in the process. In fact, I estimate that during 2025, ATAI burned through $103.6 million. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its 2025 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. But this figure suggests ATAI could eat through its liquid reserves in 2.1 years, which is an issue given that my bull case on ATAI will take at least until 2029 to fully play out.

Secondly, it's not guaranteed that ATAI will ultimately succeed with any of its candidates. And, as previously mentioned, its cash reserves can run low in just a couple of years. So, by the time we have a clearer picture of BPL-003's approval prospects, their runway will be considerably tighter. If at that point they don't have favorable trial data, it could leave investors with failed psychedelic IP and little cash to continue, which I imagine would easily translate into a much lower stock price. This is especially true since ATAI's current valuation multiples already seem considerably higher relative to its peers.

Conclusion: Long-Term Bullish

Overall, ATAI's psychedelic pipeline keeps me bullish in the end. What ultimately nudges me into a speculative "Buy" is that BPL-003's data continues to be positive, and this asset is now heading into two parallel Phase 3 trials starting Q2 2026. Moreover, the rest of their pipeline has promising assets that would be indirectly validated by BPL-003 if it reaches FDA approval at some point in the future. Plus, we do know they have enough runway for the next couple of years, so I'm not overly concerned about any major dilutive events in the near term. Thus, as long as you're willing to hold the shares for the long run, I believe ATAI could pay off nicely in the end. Just make sure you manage your position accordingly.
